bedrockSpineCompatibility.notes: Each chapter maps to one lesson day

chapters.title: ROBIN BECOMES AN OUTLAW
chapters.summary: In the green heart of England, in the days when King Henry ruled the land, there lived a young man named Robin of Locksley.  He was eighteen years old, strong and quick, with sharp eyes and a laugh that could be heard across a meadow.
chapters.text:
In the green heart of England, in the days when King Henry ruled the land, there lived a young man named Robin of Locksley. He was eighteen years old, strong and quick, with sharp eyes and a laugh that could be heard across a meadow. He was the finest archer in all of Nottinghamshire — and everyone knew it, including Robin himself.
That was the problem.
One spring morning, Robin set out through Sherwood Forest on his way to Nottingham, where the Sheriff was holding an archery contest. The prize was a fine barrel of ale and a purse of silver, and Robin intended to win both. He walked with a longbow over his shoulder, whistling, feeling like the world belonged to him.
At a crossroads he met a group of the King's foresters — rough men who patrolled the royal forest and enforced the harsh laws that protected the King's deer. In those days, killing one of the King's deer was a crime punishable by hanging, even if your family was starving. The foresters were hated by common people, and they knew it, and they didn't care.
"Where are you going, boy?" the chief forester called out. He was a big man with a red face and mean eyes.
"To Nottingham, to win the shooting match," Robin said cheerfully.
The foresters laughed. "You? You're just a beardless boy. I'll wager you couldn't hit a deer at a hundred paces."
Robin's temper flared — a weakness that would follow him all his life. "I'll bet you twenty marks I can hit any target you name."
The forester pointed to a herd of the King's deer grazing in a clearing a hundred yards away. "Hit the finest buck in that herd, and I'll pay you twenty marks."
Robin nocked an arrow, drew his great bow, and let fly. The arrow sang through the air and struck the buck cleanly. The animal fell without a sound.
The foresters' laughter died.
"You fool," the chief forester snarled. "You've killed one of the King's deer. That's a hanging offense."
Robin realized what he had done. In his pride and temper, he had committed a crime that could cost him his life. The foresters reached for their weapons.
Robin ran.
An arrow whistled past his ear. He plunged into the thick woods, crashing through brambles and leaping over fallen logs. Behind him, the foresters shouted and cursed. Another arrow hissed by, close enough to clip a leaf beside his face. Robin turned, drew his own bow, and fired back. His arrow found its mark — one of the foresters fell.
Robin stared in horror at what he had done. Then he disappeared into the deepest part of Sherwood Forest, where the oaks grew so thick that sunlight barely reached the ground.
By nightfall, Robin of Locksley was a wanted man. A price was on his head. He could never go home again.
He was eighteen years old, and his old life was over.

grammar.instruction: A simple sentence has one independent clause. A compound sentence joins two independent clauses with a conjunction (and, but, or, so). A complex sentence has one independent clause and one dependent clause.
examples: Compound: Robin fled into the forest, and the foresters chased him.
Complex: When Robin killed the deer, he committed a crime.

informationalText.title: Medieval English Law
informationalText.text:
In medieval England, forests belonged to the king. Hunting the king's deer was considered poaching—a crime punishable by death or mutilation. Common people could starve while surrounded by game they weren't allowed to touch. These harsh forest laws created widespread resentment and drove many otherwise law-abiding people to become outlaws. Robin Hood's story reflects this historical reality: one moment of impulse could turn a free person into a hunted criminal with no way back.

The foresters who enforced these laws were feared and hated. They had the power to arrest, fine, or even execute poachers on the spot. Their corruption was legendary—many took bribes or falsely accused innocent people to collect rewards. This system of injustice is why Robin Hood became a folk hero: he represented resistance against laws that protected the wealthy while oppressing the poor.

writingPrompt.prompt: Robin makes a life-changing mistake because of his pride and temper. Write about a time when an emotion (anger, pride, fear, excitement) led you to make a bad decision. What happened? What did you learn?

chapters.title: THE GREENWOOD
chapters.summary: For weeks, Robin hid in Sherwood Forest, sleeping under trees, eating what he could hunt and forage.  He was alone and afraid, but he was also free — freer than he had ever been in his life.
chapters.text:
For weeks, Robin hid in Sherwood Forest, sleeping under trees, eating what he could hunt and forage. He was alone and afraid, but he was also free — freer than he had ever been in his life.
And he was not the only outlaw in the greenwood.
Sherwood Forest was home to dozens of men who had been driven outside the law by the cruelty of the times. Some had poached deer to feed hungry families. Others had fallen into debt and been thrown off their land by greedy lords. A few were former soldiers, abandoned by the crown after years of service. They lived in the forest like ghosts, hunted by the Sheriff's men, surviving on whatever they could find.
Robin found them — or they found him. Either way, within a month he had gathered around him a band of loyal followers. They recognized something in the young outlaw: courage, fairness, and a gift for leadership that went beyond his years.
He gave them rules.
"We rob from those who have too much," Robin declared, standing on a fallen oak in a forest clearing that would become their meeting place. "We give to those who have too little. We never harm a woman or a child. We never rob a farmer, a craftsman, or any honest person who works for their bread. But lords, bishops, and tax collectors who grow fat on the suffering of common folk — they are fair game."
The men cheered.
"And one more thing," Robin added, his eyes serious. "We answer to each other. Every man here has a voice. No one is above the law of the greenwood."
They made their camp in a hidden glen deep in the forest, beneath ancient oaks so massive that six men linking hands could not circle their trunks. They built shelters of branches and moss, dug a great cooking pit, and set lookouts on every trail. They wore green — Lincoln green, the color of the forest — so they could move through the trees like shadows.
The people of Nottinghamshire began to whisper the name Robin Hood. And the Sheriff of Nottingham began to grind his teeth.

informationalText.title: Life in Sherwood Forest
informationalText.text:
Sherwood Forest in the 12th-13th centuries was an enormous woodland covering over 100,000 acres in Nottinghamshire. The forest was dense with oak, birch, and ash trees, providing perfect cover for those hiding from the law. Wild game was plentiful: deer, boar, rabbits, and birds. For outlaws, the forest offered both sanctuary and survival.

Living as an outlaw meant constant danger. You couldn't seek help from the law, visit family, or trade in towns. Outlaws formed bands for protection and survival. They built hidden camps, developed coded signals, and relied on a network of sympathetic villagers for information. The greenwood became a kingdom unto itself—outside royal law but governed by its own code of honor.

journalPrompt.prompt: Robin gives his band clear rules about who they can rob and who they must protect. Do you think stealing from the rich to give to the poor is justified? Why or why not? Are there situations where breaking the law might be the right thing to do?

chapters.title: LITTLE JOHN
chapters.summary:
Robin was walking alone through the forest one morning when he came to a narrow bridge over a rushing stream — just a single log, wide enough for one person to cross at a time. 
On the other side stood the biggest man Robin had ever seen.
chapters.text:
Robin was walking alone through the forest one morning when he came to a narrow bridge over a rushing stream — just a single log, wide enough for one person to cross at a time.
On the other side stood the biggest man Robin had ever seen. He was nearly seven feet tall, broad as a barn door, with arms like tree limbs and a quarterstaff that looked like a young oak. His name, Robin would later learn, was John Little — though everyone would soon call him Little John, as a joke that never got old.
"Step aside," Robin called. "I mean to cross this bridge."
"Step aside yourself," the big man replied. "I was here first."
Neither moved. Robin grinned. The stranger didn't.
"I'll tell you what," Robin said. "We'll settle this the old-fashioned way. Quarterstaves. Whoever knocks the other into the stream gets the bridge."
The big man shrugged. "Suits me. But you're half my size. It hardly seems fair."
Robin cut himself a staff from a nearby oak — six feet of solid wood — and they met in the middle of the log bridge, balancing over the rushing water.
They fought for nearly half an hour. Robin was faster, darting in with quick strikes, dancing back before the big man could land a blow. But Little John was immovable — every hit Robin landed felt like striking a stone wall. And when Little John swung, the air whistled.
One massive blow caught Robin across the ribs. He staggered, slipped, and tumbled into the stream with a tremendous splash.
He came up sputtering, soaked from head to toe, his dignity considerably damaged. The big man looked down at him from the bridge with a faint smile.
Robin burst out laughing.
"Well fought!" he said, hauling himself out of the water. "I haven't been beaten in years. What's your name?"
"John Little."
"Well, John Little, how would you like to join a band of merry outlaws? We live in the greenwood, we rob from the rich, and we eat better than most lords."
John thought about it for approximately two seconds. "I'm in."
Robin blew his hunting horn — three short blasts — and within minutes, his men appeared from the trees like green phantoms. They welcomed John Little with a feast, renamed him Little John, and from that day forward, he was Robin's closest friend and most trusted companion.
He never lost another fight on a bridge. Mostly because no one was foolish enough to challenge him.

informationalText.title: Medieval Weapons: The Quarterstaff
informationalText.text:
The quarterstaff was one of the most common weapons in medieval England. Made from hardwood (usually oak or ash), it measured 6-8 feet long and was thick as a broom handle. Unlike swords, which only nobles could afford, anyone could make or own a quarterstaff. This made it the weapon of common people—farmers, travelers, and outlaws.

Fighting with a quarterstaff required skill, strength, and quick reflexes. Fighters used both ends of the staff, striking from multiple angles. A good quarterstaff fighter could defeat a swordsman by keeping them at distance and striking before they could close in. Little John's mastery of this weapon made him nearly unbeatable in close combat.

writingPrompt.prompt: Robin creates rules for his band: rob from the rich, give to the poor, never harm women or children, everyone has a voice. If you were creating a code of behavior for a group, what would your top three rules be? Explain why each rule matters.

chapters.title: THE SHOOTING MATCH
chapters.summary: The Sheriff of Nottingham was furious.  Robin Hood was making him look like a fool.
chapters.text:
The Sheriff of Nottingham was furious. Robin Hood was making him look like a fool. The outlaw robbed travelers on the King's roads, gave the money to the poor, and vanished into the forest before the Sheriff's men could catch him. Worse, the common people loved Robin and helped hide him.
The Sheriff devised a plan.
"Robin Hood is vain about his archery," the Sheriff told his advisors. "We'll hold a grand shooting match in Nottingham with a prize so fine he won't be able to resist. A golden arrow. When he shows up, we'll surround him and take him."
Word of the contest spread across Nottinghamshire. The golden arrow would be awarded to the finest archer in England. Every bowman for fifty miles would compete.
Robin's men warned him. "It's a trap," said Will Stutely, one of his most loyal followers. "The Sheriff wants your head."
"Of course it's a trap," Robin said cheerfully. "That's what makes it fun."
On the day of the contest, the streets of Nottingham were packed. The Sheriff sat on a raised platform, surrounded by armed guards, scanning the crowd for Robin Hood. He was looking for a young man in Lincoln green with a feather in his cap.
What he saw was a ragged old man with a patched cloak, a bent back, and a scraggly beard — shuffling to the shooting line with a battered bow that looked like it might fall apart.
The contest went on for hours. Archer after archer stepped forward. The targets were set at increasing distances. One by one, the competitors were eliminated until only three remained: a yeoman in brown, a tall stranger in scarlet, and the ragged old beggar.
The yeoman shot first. His arrow struck the inner ring — an excellent shot. The crowd cheered.
The stranger in scarlet shot next. His arrow struck the exact center. The crowd roared.
The old beggar stepped forward. He drew his battered bow with surprising strength, aimed for a long moment, and released. His arrow split the stranger's arrow down the middle, lodging in the exact same spot.
The crowd went wild. The Sheriff, puzzled but not suspicious, awarded the golden arrow to the ragged beggar, who shuffled away into the crowd and disappeared.
That evening, an arrow thudded into the wooden post outside the Sheriff's window. Attached to it was a note:
"Thank you for the golden arrow, my lord Sheriff. It was a lovely prize. — Robin Hood."
The Sheriff's scream could be heard across Nottingham.

informationalText.title: Archery Contests in Medieval England
informationalText.text:
Archery contests were popular entertainment in medieval England. Kings and nobles held competitions to identify skilled archers for military service. The longbow was England's most powerful weapon, capable of penetrating armor at 200 yards. Archers trained from childhood to develop the strength needed to draw a 100-150 pound bow.

Prizes at archery contests included money, livestock, or valuable items like golden arrows. These contests drew competitors from across the country. Sheriffs and lords used them as recruiting opportunities—and sometimes as traps to catch wanted criminals who couldn't resist showing off their skills.

journalPrompt.prompt: After losing the fight, Robin laughs and immediately asks Little John to join his band. What does this tell you about Robin as a leader? What qualities make someone a good leader?

chapters.title: WILL SCARLET
chapters.summary: Robin was walking through the forest one afternoon when he spotted a young man striding down the path dressed in the finest scarlet silk, with a feathered cap and a jeweled sword at his hip.  He looked like a nobleman's son who had wandered into the wrong part of England.
chapters.text:
Robin was walking through the forest one afternoon when he spotted a young man striding down the path dressed in the finest scarlet silk, with a feathered cap and a jeweled sword at his hip. He looked like a nobleman's son who had wandered into the wrong part of England.
"Good day, friend," Robin called out, stepping into the path. "That's a fine outfit. What brings a gentleman to Sherwood Forest?"
The young man's hand went to his sword. "Stand aside. I have business in Nottingham and no interest in being robbed."
"Robbed? I wouldn't dream of it. But you should know that this forest is full of outlaws. A man dressed like you is an invitation."
The stranger drew his sword. "Let them come. I'll fight every one of them."
Robin drew his own. "Well, you can start with me."
They fought with swords, and Robin quickly discovered two things: the stranger was skilled, and the stranger was angry — not at Robin, but at something deeper. He fought with a fury that went beyond self-defense.
After ten minutes of hard fighting, Robin disarmed him with a quick twist of his blade. The stranger's sword went spinning into the bushes. He stood there, breathing hard, his fine scarlet outfit torn and dirty.
"Who are you?" Robin asked.
The young man's shoulders sagged. "My name is Will Gamwell. My father is dead. His lands were seized by a corrupt lord who bribed the Sheriff. I have nothing left. I'm looking for my cousin — a man named Robin of Locksley. People say he's an outlaw now."
Robin stared. "Will? Will Gamwell? It's me. It's Robin."
Will's eyes went wide. The two cousins embraced, laughing and pounding each other's backs. The band, who had been watching from the trees the entire time, came forward to welcome the newcomer.
"We'll call you Will Scarlet," Robin said, eyeing his cousin's ruined but still vivid outfit. "And we'll get you some proper Lincoln green."
"I'd rather keep the scarlet," Will said stubbornly.
He did. And he fought in it for the rest of his life.

vocabulary.definition: a man of high social rank, usually with a title and land
vocabulary.contextSentence: He looked like a nobleman's son who had wandered into the wrong part of England.

vocabulary.definition: dishonest; willing to act illegally for personal gain

vocabulary.discussionQuestion: How does corruption drive people like Will to become outlaws?

comprehension.question: Why has Will Gamwell come to Sherwood Forest?

He was sent by King Richard to spy on Robin Hood

comprehension.question: Will Scarlet is a nobleman's son who becomes an outlaw. How does his story connect to the chapter's larger theme about how corruption in power creates outlaws?

informationalText.title: Medieval Social Classes
informationalText.text:
Medieval English society was rigidly divided into classes. At the top were the king and nobles who owned land. Below them were knights who served the nobles in exchange for land and protection. The vast majority of people were peasants—farmers who worked the land but didn't own it.

Nobles like Will Scarlet's father could lose everything if a more powerful lord wanted their land. Corrupt officials used legal tricks, false debts, and bribery to seize property. Once wealthy, a family could become destitute overnight with no legal recourse. This injustice drove many educated, skilled people to join outlaw bands—bringing literacy, strategy, and fighting skills to the greenwood.

chapters.title: THE TINKER AND THE WARRANT
chapters.summary: One day, a tinker came tramping down the forest road — a big, cheerful man who mended pots and pans for a living.  His name was Middle, and he carried a heavy cudgel and a rolled-up document tucked inside his shirt.
chapters.text:
One day, a tinker came tramping down the forest road — a big, cheerful man who mended pots and pans for a living. His name was Middle, and he carried a heavy cudgel and a rolled-up document tucked inside his shirt.
Robin, disguised as a simple traveler, fell into step beside him.
"Good morning, friend. Where are you headed?"
"To find Robin Hood," the tinker said proudly. "I've got a warrant for his arrest, signed by the Sheriff himself. Fifty pounds reward if I bring him in."
Robin nearly choked. "Robin Hood! That's a dangerous man, I hear. You'd be wise to be careful."
"Careful?" The tinker laughed. "I've fought bears. I've wrestled bulls. One outlaw doesn't worry me."
Robin suggested they stop at a tavern for a drink. The tinker happily agreed. Over several mugs of ale, Robin quietly slipped the warrant out of the tinker's shirt and hid it. When the tinker finally reached for the document and found it gone, he turned on Robin in a rage.
"You stole my warrant! You're one of his men!"
"Worse than that," Robin said, grinning. "I'm Robin Hood himself."
The tinker swung his cudgel. Robin ducked. What followed was a furious fight — cudgel against sword, strength against speed — that went crashing through the tavern and out into the road. Tables were overturned. Mugs were shattered. The tavern keeper dove behind the bar and stayed there.
In the end, they fought to a draw. Both men stood panting, bruised, and grinning at each other.
"You're a fine fighter," Robin said. "Forget the warrant. Join us."
The tinker looked at the torn warrant, looked at Robin, looked at the beautiful forest stretching in every direction — and made his choice.
"The Sheriff can find someone else," he said.

clever in a sneaky or deceptive way; skill in achieving one's aims through trickery

3. RECRUIT  (verb/noun) — to persuade someone to join a group; a new member

vocabulary.discussionQuestion: What does Robin look for when he recruits new members?

informationalText.title: Medieval Trades: The Tinker
informationalText.text:
Tinkers were traveling craftsmen who mended metal pots, pans, and tools. They moved from village to village, carrying their tools and supplies on their backs or in carts. Tinkers were skilled metalworkers who could repair almost anything, making them valuable in communities where buying new items was expensive.

Because they traveled constantly, tinkers heard news and gossip from many places. Some worked as messengers or spies. Their freedom to move without suspicion made them useful to both outlaws and authorities. A tinker carrying a warrant was a perfect cover—who would suspect a simple tradesman of working for the sheriff?

writingPrompt.prompt: Robin loses the bridge fight but gains a loyal friend. Write about a situation where losing, failing, or being wrong led to something good. What did you gain from that experience?

chapters.title: FRIAR TUCK
chapters.summary: Robin had heard rumors of a friar living near Fountain Abbey — a holy man who was also, supposedly, the best swordsman in the county.  Robin had to see for himself.
chapters.text:
Robin had heard rumors of a friar living near Fountain Abbey — a holy man who was also, supposedly, the best swordsman in the county. Robin had to see for himself.
He found the friar sitting by a stream, roasting a chicken. Friar Tuck was not what Robin expected. He was round and red-faced, with a bald head ringed by a fringe of brown hair. He wore the brown robes of his order, but beneath them were the shoulders and arms of a fighter. A broadsword hung at his side — unusual equipment for a man of God.
"Good friar," Robin said politely, "I need to cross this stream, and I don't wish to get my feet wet. Would you be so kind as to carry me across on your back?"
The friar looked Robin up and down. "Very well," he said mildly. He hoisted Robin onto his broad back, waded into the stream — and halfway across, dumped him into the water.
Robin came up sputtering. "What was that for?"
"You looked like you needed a bath," the friar said.
Robin demanded the friar carry him back. The friar agreed — and dumped him again. This went on three times until both men were soaked, furious, and laughing so hard they could barely stand.
Then they fought. Swords clashed, water sprayed, and for an hour they battled back and forth across the stream, neither able to gain the upper hand. Friar Tuck was faster than his round body suggested, and he fought with a joyful ferocity that delighted Robin even as it bruised him.
"Enough!" Robin finally called. "You're the finest fighting man I've met since Little John. Come join us in the greenwood. We need a chaplain — and apparently a swimming instructor."
The friar considered this. He looked at his lonely campfire, his half-eaten chicken, and the empty forest around him. He had been living alone for years, cast out by the Church for his temper and his unwillingness to bow to corrupt bishops.
"Will there be regular meals?" he asked.
"The best venison in England."
"I'm your man."
And so Friar Tuck joined the band — bringing with him his sword, his appetite, and three dogs that followed him everywhere.

informationalText.title: Medieval Monasteries and Friars
informationalText.text:
In medieval England, monasteries were centers of learning, medicine, and hospitality. Monks and friars took vows of poverty, chastity, and obedience. Friars, unlike monks, traveled and preached among common people rather than staying in cloistered monasteries.

However, not all religious men were holy. Some abbots and bishops grew wealthy and corrupt, forgetting their vows. Friars who spoke out against this corruption were sometimes cast out of the church. These "rogue friars" often had more in common with outlaws than with church officials. Friar Tuck represents this type: a genuine man of faith who couldn't stomach the church's hypocrisy.

journalPrompt.prompt: The Sheriff sets a trap specifically designed around Robin's vanity about archery. What does this teach us about how weaknesses can be used against us? How can we protect ourselves from people who know our weaknesses?

chapters.title: THE SHERIFF'S FEAST
chapters.summary: Robin loved a good disguise.  It was his favorite weapon — more useful than any bow or sword.
chapters.text:
Robin loved a good disguise. It was his favorite weapon — more useful than any bow or sword. He could be a beggar, a merchant, a potter, or a monk. He changed his voice, his walk, his entire manner. The Sheriff had posted Robin's description in every village in Nottinghamshire, but descriptions were useless against a man who could become anyone.
One market day, Robin dressed as a butcher — a broad-faced, cheerful fellow with a blood-stained apron and a cart full of meat. He drove into Nottingham, set up a stall, and began selling at prices so low that every housewife in the city came running.
"Best beef in England! Half price today only!"
The other butchers were furious. The customers were delighted. And the Sheriff's wife, hearing about the bargains, sent a servant to buy their entire dinner from the generous newcomer.
The butcher was so charming, so funny, and so generous that the Sheriff himself invited him to dinner at Nottingham Castle. Robin accepted with a deep bow.
He sat at the Sheriff's own table, ate the Sheriff's food, drank the Sheriff's wine, and told the Sheriff outrageous stories about his "cattle ranch" deep in Sherwood Forest. The Sheriff, who was greedy above all things, grew very interested.
"You say you have hundreds of head of cattle? In the forest?"
"Five hundred at least, my lord. Fine beasts, every one. I'd sell the whole herd for a fair price."
"Show me," the Sheriff said, his eyes gleaming with greed.
Robin led the Sheriff and a small escort deep into Sherwood Forest — deeper and deeper, until the trees closed overhead and the paths disappeared. Then Robin put his fingers to his lips and blew three sharp blasts on his hunting horn.
Green-clad men appeared from behind every tree.
"Welcome to my cattle ranch, my lord Sheriff," Robin said, dropping the butcher's accent. "I'm Robin Hood. And you are my guest."
The Sheriff's face went white. But Robin was true to his code — he didn't harm the Sheriff. He simply took every coin the man was carrying, fed him a fine dinner of roast venison, and sent him home on a donkey, with the laughter of the Merry Men ringing in his ears.
The Sheriff did not find it funny.

a role or character adopted by a person, especially one that is different from their true self

informationalText.title: Medieval Disguises
informationalText.text:
In an age before photographs or widespread literacy, disguises were remarkably effective. Most people couldn't read wanted posters, and descriptions were vague: "tall," "dark-haired," "bearded." Changing clothes, posture, and accent could make someone unrecognizable.

Successful disguises required more than costume—they required acting. Robin Hood was a master of this. He could mimic the speech patterns, mannerisms, and attitudes of different social classes. A beggar walked differently than a merchant. A butcher talked differently than a knight. By observing carefully and practicing, Robin could become anyone.

writingPrompt.prompt: Robin knows the archery contest is a trap but goes anyway, saying "if I don't go, he wins." Write about a time when you had to face something difficult or risky even though it would have been easier to avoid it. What made you decide to go forward?

chapters.title: ALLAN-A-DALE
chapters.summary: A young man came walking through the forest one morning, and Robin knew something was wrong before the stranger said a word.  He was young, barely twenty, and he carried a lute on his back.
chapters.text:
A young man came walking through the forest one morning, and Robin knew something was wrong before the stranger said a word. He was young, barely twenty, and he carried a lute on his back. His face was handsome but hollow with grief, and he walked like a man who no longer cared where his feet took him.
"Good morning, friend," Robin said, stepping out from behind a tree. "You look like a man who's lost something."
"I've lost everything," the young man said quietly. His name was Allan-a-Dale, and he was a minstrel — a traveling musician. He had fallen in love with a young woman named Ellen, and she loved him back. They had planned to marry.
But Ellen's father owed money to a wealthy old knight named Sir Stephen of Trent. To pay the debt, the father had promised Ellen to Sir Stephen as a bride. The wedding was set for that very afternoon, at the church in Plympton, and there was nothing Allan could do to stop it.
"She doesn't love him," Allan said, his voice breaking. "He's sixty years old and cruel. She wept when she told me. But her father says the debt must be paid."
Robin's jaw tightened. "Where is this church?"
One hour later, Robin Hood and a dozen of his men appeared at the church in Plympton. Robin was disguised as a wandering harper. Little John stood guard at the door. Friar Tuck — an actual ordained friar — stood ready.
The ceremony had already begun. Sir Stephen, old and richly dressed, stood at the altar beside Ellen, who was pale and trembling. The bishop was reading the vows.
Robin stepped forward and began to play his harp — loudly.
"What is the meaning of this?" the bishop demanded.
"A small change of plans," Robin said. He threw off his disguise, and his men blocked every exit. Ellen's father protested. Sir Stephen sputtered with rage.
Robin turned to Ellen. "Do you love this man?" he asked, pointing to Sir Stephen.
"No," she whispered.
"Do you love Allan-a-Dale?"
"With all my heart."
Robin turned to Friar Tuck. "Marry them."
Tuck performed the ceremony right there, over the bishop's objections and Sir Stephen's threats. Allan and Ellen were married before sunset. Robin paid off Ellen's father's debt with money taken from the bishop's own saddlebags.
Allan-a-Dale joined the band that day, becoming their minstrel. His songs about Robin Hood would be sung for centuries.

vocabulary.definition: a medieval musician who traveled from place to place, singing songs and telling stories
vocabulary.contextSentence: His name was Allan-a-Dale, and he was a minstrel—a traveling musician.

to become involved in a situation in order to change it

4. ORDAINED  (adjective/verb) — officially appointed as a priest or minister through a religious ceremony

informationalText.title: Medieval Marriage and Debt
informationalText.text:
In medieval England, marriage among wealthy families was about property and alliances, not love. Fathers arranged their daughters' marriages to benefit the family. Young women had almost no say in whom they married. Refusing could mean disinheritance or worse.

Debt was a powerful weapon. A wealthy man could loan money at high interest, knowing the borrower could never repay. When debt came due, the lender could demand land, possessions, or even a family member in marriage. This practice was legal but deeply immoral—which is why stories like Allan-a-Dale's wedding rescue resonated with common people who had seen it happen.

journalPrompt.prompt: Will Scarlet refuses to change out of his red outfit even though everyone else wears green. Why do you think he does this? Is it important to maintain your individual identity even when you're part of a group?

chapters.title: THE GOLDEN ARROW
chapters.summary: The Sheriff had not forgotten his humiliation.  He was a proud man, and Robin Hood had made a fool of him more times than he could count.
chapters.text:
The Sheriff had not forgotten his humiliation. He was a proud man, and Robin Hood had made a fool of him more times than he could count. He devised another plan — a second archery contest, but this time with a much more elaborate trap.
The prize was a golden arrow on a silver shaft. The contest would be held in a field outside Nottingham, surrounded by the Sheriff's men — hundreds of them, armed and ready. Every road into the city would be watched. Every traveler would be searched.
"This time," the Sheriff told his captain, "Robin Hood will not escape."
Robin's scouts brought word of the trap. Little John counseled caution. "Don't go, Robin. He's laid this one carefully."
"If I don't go, he wins," Robin said. "And I never let the Sheriff win."
But Robin didn't walk into the trap blindly. He sent his men into Nottingham in twos and threes over several days, disguised as farmers, merchants, and monks. By the day of the contest, more than forty of Robin's men were scattered through the crowd, armed and ready.
Robin himself came disguised as a one-eyed beggar, so convincing that his own men had to look twice to recognize him.
The contest was fierce. Archers from across England competed. The Sheriff watched eagerly, certain that Robin would reveal himself.
In the final round, the one-eyed beggar stepped to the line. He drew a battered bow, aimed with his one good eye, and put his arrow into the exact center of the target.
Silence. Then thunderous applause.
The Sheriff studied the beggar suspiciously but saw nothing familiar. He awarded the golden arrow.
As before, Robin waited until he was safely back in the forest before sending the Sheriff a mocking message. But this time, the Sheriff had planned ahead. He had men following every suspicious person who left the tournament. They tracked Robin's men to the edge of the forest and launched an ambush.
Arrows flew. Steel clashed. Two of Robin's men were wounded before the band could melt back into the deep woods. It was the closest the Sheriff had ever come to catching them.
Robin sat by the campfire that night, cleaning a wound on his arm, and for the first time, he didn't laugh.

informationalText.title: Sheriffs in Medieval England
informationalText.text:
Sheriffs were the king's representatives in each county, responsible for collecting taxes, enforcing laws, and maintaining order. It was one of the most powerful positions in medieval England—and one of the most corrupt. Sheriffs could enrich themselves by overtaxing, accepting bribes, and seizing property.

The Sheriff of Nottingham in Robin Hood tales represents this corruption. He persecutes outlaws not from duty but from wounded pride and desire for reward money. He uses his legal authority for personal vengeance. This made him the perfect villain in folk stories: someone with legitimate power who uses it unjustly.

chapters.title: GUY OF GISBOURNE
chapters.summary: The Sheriff realized he could not catch Robin Hood with soldiers.  He needed someone who could match Robin skill for skill, cunning for cunning.
chapters.text:
The Sheriff realized he could not catch Robin Hood with soldiers. He needed someone who could match Robin skill for skill, cunning for cunning. He needed an assassin.
He found Guy of Gisbourne.
Guy was a knight who had been stripped of his lands for acts of violence so extreme that even the other nobles were disgusted. He was tall, lean, and deadly — a master swordsman and archer who wore a cloak of rough horsehide and carried a black-handled dagger that had killed more men than anyone could count. He had no loyalty except to money, and no mercy at all.
"Kill Robin Hood," the Sheriff said. "Bring me his head. I'll pay you his weight in silver."
"Done," said Guy of Gisbourne.
Guy entered Sherwood Forest alone, something no hired killer had dared before. He moved quietly, watching, listening, reading the forest like a hunter reads tracks. He was patient. He was dangerous.
Robin found him — or rather, they found each other, meeting on a narrow forest path as if the forest itself had arranged it.
They talked first. Both men took the measure of the other — the careful way a swordsman studies an opponent before the first blow. Guy pretended to be a traveler. Robin pretended to believe him.
Then the pretending stopped.
The fight was unlike anything Robin had experienced. Guy was fast, brutal, and absolutely without hesitation. He fought to kill — no honor, no rules, no pauses. His sword cut through Robin's sleeve, drawing blood. His dagger nearly found Robin's throat.
Robin had always fought with joy — the thrill of the contest, the pleasure of testing himself. Against Guy of Gisbourne, there was no joy. There was only survival.
The fight lasted twenty minutes. It ended when Robin caught Guy's sword arm in a bind, twisted, and drove his own blade home. Guy fell.
Robin stood over him, breathing hard, blood running down his arm. He looked at the dead man's face and felt nothing but a cold, hollow relief.
He took Guy's horsehide cloak and wore it into Nottingham, disguised as the assassin. When the Sheriff eagerly asked for Robin Hood's body, Robin revealed himself, mocked the Sheriff one final time, and escaped in the chaos.
But the fight with Guy stayed with him. For the first time, Robin understood that this life — however merry they called it — had real consequences. People were trying to kill him, and someday, someone might succeed.

informationalText.title: Medieval Assassins
informationalText.text:
Medieval England had professional fighters-for-hire called "knightly mercenaries." These were skilled warriors who fought for whoever paid them. Some were former knights who had lost their lands or been disgraced. With no loyalty except to money, they were dangerous and ruthless.

Guy of Gisbourne represents this type: a skilled fighter with no moral compass. The text describes him wearing horsehide (an unusual choice that suggests wildness) and carrying weapons designed only for killing. Unlike Robin's band, who rob but rarely kill, Guy has no code. This makes him more dangerous than any sheriff's army.

writingPrompt.prompt: Will Scarlet loses his home and wealth but finds a new family with Robin's band. Write about a time when losing something (moving away, losing a friend, ending an activity) led to finding something new and valuable.

chapters.title: MAID MARIAN
chapters.summary:
Robin Hood had a secret that none of the ballad singers ever got quite right. 
Before he became an outlaw — before the deer, the foresters, and the flight into the greenwood — Robin of Locksley had been in love.
chapters.text:
Robin Hood had a secret that none of the ballad singers ever got quite right.
Before he became an outlaw — before the deer, the foresters, and the flight into the greenwood — Robin of Locksley had been in love. Her name was Marian Fitzwalter, and she was the daughter of a minor nobleman whose estate bordered the forest. They had grown up together, climbing trees and shooting at targets in the meadow behind her father's house. She was better than Robin with a short bow, though he would never admit it publicly.
When Robin was declared an outlaw, Marian's father forbade her from seeing him. He was afraid — not of Robin, but of the Sheriff, who punished anyone suspected of helping the outlaw. For two years, Robin and Marian saw each other only in stolen moments — a meeting at the forest's edge at dawn, a message carried by a trusted friend, a glimpse across a crowded market square.
But Marian was not the kind of woman who waited quietly.
She had been feeding information to Robin for months. She knew which roads the tax collectors would travel. She knew when the Sheriff planned his raids. She had a network of servants, farmers, and village women who passed messages through the countryside like a human telegraph.
One autumn afternoon, Robin was walking in the forest when he encountered a slim young knight in full armor — visor down, sword drawn. The knight challenged Robin to a fight. Robin accepted, because Robin always accepted.
They fought with swords, and Robin was startled by how good the knight was. Quick, precise, and relentlessly aggressive. Robin couldn't find an opening. Every attack was parried. Every feint was read.
After fifteen minutes, Robin finally landed a blow that knocked the knight's helmet off.
A cascade of dark hair tumbled out. Marian glared at him, breathing hard, a thin cut on her cheek from where Robin's blade had grazed her.
"You've gotten slower," she said.
Robin stared. Then he laughed until he couldn't breathe.
From that day forward, Marian was part of the band — not as a decoration or a damsel, but as a fighter, a strategist, and Robin's equal. She could outshoot half the men, outthink most of them, and out-argue all of them, including Robin.
The Merry Men learned quickly: when Robin Hood wanted to know what to do, he asked Marian Fitzwalter.

to think something is less important, powerful, or capable than it really is
From the text: Robin doesn't recognize the knight as Marian because he underestimates what she can do.
Who else in the story underestimates Marian, and why?
4. EQUAL  (noun/adjective) — a person with the same abilities, status, or rights as another
vocabulary.contextSentence: Robin's equal.
vocabulary.discussionQuestion: What does it mean for Marian to be Robin's equal in a time when women had very few rights?

informationalText.title: Women in Medieval England
informationalText.text:
Medieval women were expected to be obedient daughters, then obedient wives. They couldn't own property, make contracts, or hold most positions of power. Education was rare for women except in noble families. A woman's value was often measured by her father's wealth or her ability to produce sons.

But reality was more complex than the rules. Some women managed estates while husbands were away at war. Others ran businesses, practiced medicine, or influenced politics indirectly. Maid Marian represents an idealized version of this: a woman who refuses to be limited by society's expectations and proves herself equal to any man.

journalPrompt.prompt: The tinker was cheerful and friendly even while planning to capture Robin for money. Can someone be a good person even if they're doing something wrong? Where do you draw the line?

chapters.title: THE BISHOP'S RANSOM
chapters.summary: Winter came to Sherwood Forest, and with it, hardship.  The ground froze.
chapters.text:
Winter came to Sherwood Forest, and with it, hardship. The ground froze. Game grew scarce. The band huddled around smoky fires, mending their clothes and sharpening their weapons. Even Robin's good humor was tested by the cold.
Then word came from Marian's network: the Bishop of Hereford was traveling through Sherwood Forest with a wagon full of gold — tax money collected from the poorest villages in the county, bound for the King's treasury. The Bishop traveled with only a small escort, confident that his holy office would protect him.
He was wrong.
Robin and twenty of his men set an ambush at a narrow bend in the road where the forest pressed close on both sides. They dropped a tree across the path and waited.
The Bishop's wagon appeared, creaking under the weight of its cargo. Six armed guards rode alongside. The Bishop himself sat in the wagon, fat and comfortable, wrapped in furs against the cold.
"Good morning, Your Grace," Robin said, stepping out of the trees. "Terrible weather for traveling. Perhaps you'd like to rest a while."
The guards reached for their swords. Little John stepped out behind them, his quarterstaff across his shoulders. Friar Tuck appeared on the other side of the road. Green-clad men rose from behind every bush and rock.
The guards lowered their weapons.
Robin didn't take everything — that was his rule. He took half the gold and left the Bishop enough to continue his journey. But before letting him go, Robin made the Bishop sit on a log and eat a simple meal of bread and cold venison — the same food the peasants lived on.
"This is what the people you tax eat every day, Your Grace," Robin said. "Remember it."
The Bishop left Sherwood Forest in a fury, vowing revenge. But the gold Robin took fed three villages through the winter. Children who would have gone hungry ate bread. Families who couldn't pay their rent were spared eviction.
Friar Tuck, who had his own opinions about corrupt churchmen, said a prayer over the gold before it was distributed. "The Lord works in mysterious ways," he said. "Sometimes those ways involve highway robbery."

vocabulary.definition:
a sum of money demanded for the release of a prisoner; also, money taken by force
From the text: Robin takes half the Bishop's gold as a ransom for passage through his forest.
How is Robin's ransom different from a criminal's ransom?
2. DISTRIBUTION  (noun) — the act of sharing or giving out something to a group
vocabulary.contextSentence: The gold Robin took fed three villages through the winter.
vocabulary.discussionQuestion: Why is the distribution of the gold important to Robin's mission?

vocabulary.definition: the act of forcing someone to leave their home
vocabulary.contextSentence: Families who couldn't pay their rent were spared eviction.
vocabulary.discussionQuestion: What makes eviction especially cruel during winter?

informationalText.title: The Medieval Church and Taxes
informationalText.text:
The Catholic Church was immensely powerful and wealthy in medieval England. It collected tithes (10% of everyone's income) and owned vast amounts of land. Bishops lived like nobles, with castles, servants, and private armies. This wealth came from the poorest people, who were taught that giving to the church ensured salvation.

Many religious leaders were genuinely devoted, but corruption was widespread. Some bishops taxed ruthlessly, forgave sins for payment, and lived in luxury while preaching poverty. This hypocrisy angered common people and made corrupt churchmen targets for outlaws like Robin Hood, who saw them as thieves in holy robes.

writingPrompt.prompt: The tinker was hunting Robin for reward money but becomes his friend instead. Write about a time when your first impression of someone was wrong. How did your opinion change, and what caused it?

chapters.title: THE POOR KNIGHT
chapters.summary: Not all of Robin's encounters involved robbery.  Some involved justice of a different kind.
chapters.text:
Not all of Robin's encounters involved robbery. Some involved justice of a different kind.
Sir Richard of the Lea was a knight — an honest man who had fallen on hard times. His son had accidentally killed a man in a tournament, and Sir Richard had borrowed four hundred pounds from the Abbot of St. Mary's to pay the legal fees. The Abbot had charged ruinous interest, and now the debt had grown to a sum Sir Richard could never repay. If he didn't pay by Michaelmas — just three weeks away — the Abbot would seize his lands, his home, and everything he owned.
Sir Richard was riding through Sherwood Forest, head bowed, when Robin's men stopped him. They brought him to the camp, and Robin — following his custom of feeding every traveler before deciding whether to rob them — offered the knight dinner.
Over roast venison, Sir Richard told his story. Robin listened carefully.
"How much do you owe?" Robin asked.
"Four hundred pounds. I have seventeen."
Robin looked at Little John. Little John looked at Marian. Marian nodded.
"We'll lend you the money," Robin said.
Sir Richard stared. "You'll lend me four hundred pounds?"
"We took it from people who stole it first. At least this way it goes to someone who deserves it. Pay us back when you can. No interest."
Robin's men counted out four hundred pounds in silver and packed it in the knight's saddlebags. They gave him a new horse, a new cloak, and Little John as an escort to make sure he arrived safely.
Sir Richard rode to St. Mary's Abbey and paid the Abbot in full. The Abbot, who had been counting on seizing the knight's lands, was furious.
Sir Richard kept his home. And one year later, he rode back into Sherwood Forest and repaid Robin every penny — along with a gift of a hundred longbows and a hundred sheaves of arrows, the finest money could buy.
"You saved my family," Sir Richard said.
"That's what the greenwood is for," Robin replied.

vocabulary.contextSentence: The Abbot had charged ruinous interest.
vocabulary.discussionQuestion: What makes the interest ruinous rather than just expensive?

to use someone or something unfairly for one's own advantage
From the text: The Abbot exploits Sir Richard's desperation by charging extreme interest.
How does the Abbot exploit the knight's situation?
3. GENEROSITY  (noun) — the quality of being willing to give freely

examples: Little John was strong as an ox.

informationalText.title: Medieval Knights and Honor
informationalText.text:
Knights were mounted warriors who served nobles in exchange for land. They trained from childhood in combat and followed a code of chivalry: protect the weak, keep your word, show courage, and serve your lord faithfully. In theory, knights were noble warriors. In practice, many were brutal and self-serving.

Sir Richard of the Lea represents the ideal: an honest knight who fell victim to circumstances beyond his control. His story shows how the medieval system could destroy even honorable people through debt and corrupt courts. Robin's help restores what should never have been taken—justice that the legal system failed to provide.

journalPrompt.prompt: Friar Tuck is both a holy man and a skilled fighter. Can someone be both religious and violent? Are there situations where fighting is justified even for someone who preaches peace?

chapters.title: THE SILVER ARROW
chapters.summary: Spring returned, and with it, another of the Sheriff's attempts to lure Robin into a trap.  This time the prize was a silver arrow, and this time the Sheriff had hired professional archers from London — men who had never seen Robin Hood and wouldn't recognize him.
chapters.text:
Spring returned, and with it, another of the Sheriff's attempts to lure Robin into a trap. This time the prize was a silver arrow, and this time the Sheriff had hired professional archers from London — men who had never seen Robin Hood and wouldn't recognize him.
Robin went anyway. He couldn't help himself.
But Marian went with a plan. She positioned scouts at every exit from the tournament field. She arranged horses at three hidden points outside the city walls. She had Allan-a-Dale station himself in the crowd with a group of Robin's men, ready to create a distraction if things went wrong.
Robin competed disguised as a tanner — a leather worker with rough hands and a simple brown tunic. He shot brilliantly, as always, and won the silver arrow.
But the Sheriff had learned from his mistakes. This time, he didn't wait for Robin to reveal himself. The moment the tanner won, guards moved to surround the field. Every gate was locked.
"Something's wrong," Robin murmured to Allan-a-Dale.
Allan began to play his lute and sing — loudly, badly, and with great enthusiasm. The crowd gathered around him. In the confusion, Robin slipped through a gap in the guards and made it to the wall, where Will Scarlet was waiting with a rope.
They were over the wall and on horseback before the Sheriff realized what had happened. But it was close — closer than the last time. An arrow grazed Robin's shoulder as he rode. Another killed the horse behind him.
Back in the forest, Marian tended Robin's wound and said what no one else dared: "You're getting reckless. You go to these contests because you can't resist showing off. One day your pride will get you killed."
Robin opened his mouth to argue. Then he closed it. She was right, and they both knew it.

vocabulary.definition:
the observation of an area to gather information, especially before a military operation
From the text: Marian positions scouts at every exit and arranges escape routes.
How does Marian's reconnaissance improve Robin's chances of survival?
2. DISTRACTION  (noun) — something that draws attention away from the main focus
From the text: Allan-a-Dale creates a distraction by singing loudly and badly.
Why is a distraction sometimes more effective than fighting?
3. RECKLESS  (adjective) — acting without thinking about danger or consequences
vocabulary.contextSentence: You're getting reckless. You go to these contests because you can't resist showing off.
vocabulary.discussionQuestion: Is Marian right that Robin is being reckless? What evidence supports her claim?

choices: She tells him the Sheriff has hired a new assassin

comprehension.question: Marian tells Robin, "One day your pride will get you killed." Based on what you've read so far, do you think she's right? Trace the pattern of Robin's pride causing danger across multiple chapters.

informationalText.title: Medieval Pride and Downfall
informationalText.text:
In medieval thinking, pride was one of the seven deadly sins. It meant excessive belief in your own abilities and importance. Pride led people to take foolish risks, refuse good advice, and make enemies unnecessarily. It was considered especially dangerous because proud people didn't recognize their own flaw.

Robin Hood's pride in his archery is both his greatest strength and his most dangerous weakness. It makes him the best archer in England—but it also makes him reckless. He takes unnecessary risks to prove his skill, ignoring warnings from wiser friends. The text shows that even heroes have flaws, and those flaws have consequences.

chapters.title: KING RICHARD
chapters.summary:
A remarkable rumor reached Sherwood Forest: King Richard himself was coming to Nottinghamshire. 
King Richard the Lionheart had been away for years — first on Crusade, then imprisoned in Austria, then fighting to reclaim his throne from his scheming brother, Prince John.
chapters.text:
A remarkable rumor reached Sherwood Forest: King Richard himself was coming to Nottinghamshire.
King Richard the Lionheart had been away for years — first on Crusade, then imprisoned in Austria, then fighting to reclaim his throne from his scheming brother, Prince John. During his absence, men like the Sheriff of Nottingham had grown powerful, enriching themselves at the expense of the common people.
Now the King was back, and he wanted to see this Robin Hood for himself.
Richard came to Sherwood Forest in disguise — dressed as a simple abbot traveling with a small group of monks. Robin's men stopped them on the road, as they stopped everyone.
"Good day, Father Abbot," Robin said with a bow. "Welcome to Sherwood Forest. We have a tradition here — every traveler shares a meal with us, and wealthy travelers share a portion of their gold."
The disguised King played along. He dined with Robin, drank with the Merry Men, and watched their archery contests with growing admiration. Robin's men put on a demonstration — shooting at small targets from impossible distances, splitting willow wands, even shooting at each other's arrows.
Then the "abbot" challenged Robin to a boxing match.
Robin laughed — but he accepted. The abbot threw off his cloak, and Robin discovered that this particular man of God had the shoulders of a warrior and a punch like a battering ram. The disguised King knocked Robin flat with one blow.
The Merry Men went for their swords. Little John stepped forward, quarterstaff raised.
Robin held up his hand, grinning from the ground. "Hold! A fair hit. This is no ordinary abbot."
He looked up at the big man standing over him — the broad shoulders, the commanding presence, the way his "monks" all carried themselves like trained soldiers.
"Your Majesty?" Robin said quietly.

comprehension.question: How does King Richard first encounter Robin Hood?
choices: He summons Robin to London for a meeting
He comes to Sherwood disguised as an abbot and is stopped by Robin's men
The Sheriff brings Robin to the King in chains

choices: Robin defeats the King in a sword fight

comprehension.question: The King says he's heard two stories about Robin — the Sheriff's and the people's. Why do these two stories about the same person differ so much? What determines which version someone believes?

grammar.instruction: Past tense shows completed action. Present shows action happening now. Future shows action that will happen.

informationalText.title: King Richard the Lionheart
informationalText.text:
Richard I (1189-1199) was king of England but spent only six months of his ten-year reign in the country. He was a legendary warrior who led the Third Crusade, fought to reclaim his throne, and died in battle. During his absence, England was poorly governed by corrupt officials and his scheming brother John.

Richard's return in Robin Hood stories represents the fantasy of a just king setting things right. In reality, Richard cared little about England except as a source of money for his wars. But the legend of Richard as the "good king" contrasted with "bad king John" became a powerful part of English folklore.

writingPrompt.prompt: Friar Tuck left the church because he couldn't tolerate corruption and hypocrisy. Write about a time when you had to stand up for what was right even though it cost you something. What made you do it?

chapters.title: THE KING'S PARDON
chapters.summary: King Richard threw back his hood and revealed himself.  The Merry Men dropped to their knees.
chapters.text:
King Richard threw back his hood and revealed himself. The Merry Men dropped to their knees. Even Little John bowed — and Little John bowed to no one.
"Rise," the King said. "I didn't come here to be worshiped. I came to see the truth."
The truth, as Richard explained, was complicated. While he had been away, his brother Prince John and corrupt officials like the Sheriff had bled the country dry. The common people were suffering. And the one man who had stood against the injustice — Robin Hood — was a wanted criminal.
"I've heard two stories about you, Robin Hood," the King said, settling by the fire. "The Sheriff says you're a murderer, a thief, and an enemy of the crown. The common people say you're the only honest man in Nottinghamshire. Which is it?"
"A bit of both, Your Majesty," Robin said honestly. "I've stolen. I've fought. I killed a man the day I became an outlaw, and I've never stopped regretting it. But everything I've taken has gone to people who needed it more than those I took it from."
The King listened. He questioned Little John, Friar Tuck, Allan-a-Dale, and Marian. He heard about the poor knight, the forced wedding, the starving villages fed with the Bishop's gold. He heard about the Sheriff's cruelty, the unjust taxes, the families driven from their homes.
When he had heard enough, Richard stood.
"Robin of Locksley, I pardon you and every member of your band. Your crimes are forgiven. The Sheriff of Nottingham will answer for his own."
A cheer went up that shook the ancient oaks of Sherwood Forest.
"But," the King continued, and the cheering stopped. "I need men I can trust at court. Robin, I want you to come to London. Serve me as you've served these people. Bring your skills, your courage, and your loyalty to the crown."
Robin looked at his men. He looked at Marian. He looked at the green canopy of Sherwood Forest — the only home he had known for years.
"Yes, Your Majesty," he said.
He didn't know it then, but it was the beginning of the end.

vocabulary.discussionQuestion: What makes a royal pardon different from simply being found not guilty?

language.instruction: Metaphors compare WITHOUT "like" or "as." Says one thing IS another.

informationalText.title: Medieval Pardons
informationalText.text:
Kings had the power to pardon criminals—even those guilty of serious crimes. Pardons were sometimes granted as rewards for service, sometimes sold for money, and sometimes given to win popularity. A royal pardon could restore citizenship, property, and legal rights.

But pardons came with expectations. A pardoned outlaw owed the king loyalty and service. Refusing could be seen as treason. Robin's pardon puts him in an impossible position: accept and lose his freedom, or refuse and insult the king. The story shows that even well-intentioned authority can trap those it claims to help.

journalPrompt.prompt: Robin constantly uses tricks and disguises to outsmart his enemies. Is trickery and deception wrong, or does it depend on who you're deceiving and why?

chapters.title: LIFE AT COURT
chapters.summary: Robin tried.  He really did.
chapters.text:
Robin tried. He really did.
He went to London. He wore fine clothes. He attended banquets and councils and ceremonies. He smiled at lords who had grown rich by cheating peasants. He bowed to bishops who owned more land than the farmers they were supposed to care for. He stood in stone halls and breathed stale air and pretended that this was where he belonged.
It was a disaster.
Robin Hood was built for the greenwood — for open sky and fresh air, for the crack of a bowstring and the laughter of friends, for the freedom of a man who answered to no one but his own conscience. Court life was the opposite of everything he loved.
The other nobles despised him. To them, he was a common criminal who had been elevated above his station by a sentimental king. They whispered behind his back, blocked his proposals, and excluded him from every decision that mattered.
Robin's men fared no better. Little John nearly started a brawl with a duke who insulted the common people. Friar Tuck got into a loud theological argument with the Archbishop of Canterbury. Will Scarlet's scarlet outfit was mocked by every courtier in London.
Only Marian adapted. She had grown up in the world of minor nobility and understood its rules — even if she despised them. She navigated the court with skill, gathering information, making quiet alliances, and keeping Robin from punching anyone.
But even Marian could see the truth: Robin was dying inside. The light in his eyes — that restless, joyful spark — was fading. He spent more and more time staring out of windows, looking north toward the forests he had left behind.
"Go," Marian finally said, one evening as they stood on the castle walls.
"The King—"
"The King gave you a pardon, not a leash. You've served him faithfully for over a year. Your heart is in Sherwood, Robin. And so is mine."

vocabulary.discussionQuestion: Why does Robin find court life suffocating when others would see it as a privilege?

grammar.instruction: Quotation marks show exact words. Commas and periods go inside.

informationalText.title: Medieval Court Life
informationalText.text:
Royal courts were centers of power, intrigue, and complex social rules. Courtiers competed for the king's favor through flattery, gifts, and political maneuvering. Success at court required education, refined manners, fashionable clothes, and the ability to navigate complex social hierarchies.

For someone like Robin Hood—used to the freedom and honesty of the greenwood—court life was suffocating. Every word was calculated. Enemies wore smiles. Decisions were made through whispered conversations rather than open debate. The text shows that physical chains aren't the only kind of prison.

writingPrompt.prompt: Robin is a master of disguise—he can become anyone. If you could disguise yourself and walk unrecognized through your school or community for a day, what would you want to see or learn? Why?

chapters.title: RETURN TO SHERWOOD
chapters.summary: They left London before dawn, without permission and without farewell.  Robin, Marian, Little John, Friar Tuck, Will Scarlet, Allan-a-Dale, and a dozen others rode north through the spring rain, shedding their fine clothes mile by mile until they were back in Lincoln green.
chapters.text:
They left London before dawn, without permission and without farewell. Robin, Marian, Little John, Friar Tuck, Will Scarlet, Allan-a-Dale, and a dozen others rode north through the spring rain, shedding their fine clothes mile by mile until they were back in Lincoln green.
When they reached the edge of Sherwood Forest, Robin stopped his horse and breathed deep. The smell of oak and moss and wet earth filled his lungs. A thrush sang from a high branch. Somewhere deep in the trees, a deer moved through the undergrowth.
He was home.
Word spread quickly. Within a week, the band was back to full strength. Men who had tried to return to normal life drifted back to the greenwood — farmers who couldn't pay their taxes, soldiers who couldn't find work, young men who wanted something more than servitude and silence.
Robin's return was not welcome everywhere. The new Sheriff of Nottingham — the old one had been removed by King Richard, but his replacement was no better — immediately sent word to London that the outlaw had broken his pardon.
Robin didn't care. He was done with pardons and politics and the suffocating world of power. He picked up his bow, walked into the forest, and began again.
Marian walked beside him. "No regrets?" she asked.
"Not one," he said.
And for a while, the old days returned. The laughter in the greenwood, the adventures on the road, the songs around the fire at night. Allan-a-Dale composed new ballads. Friar Tuck blessed the venison. Little John challenged every stranger to a fight on the bridge, and usually won.
But the world was changing. Robin was no longer eighteen. And time, which spares no one, was beginning to make itself felt.

vocabulary.definition:
the act of beginning something again after a pause; restoring to a fresh condition
From the text: Robin returns to the greenwood and the band reassembles.
What aspects of Robin's life undergo renewal when he returns?
2. DEFIANCE  (noun) — open resistance; bold disobedience
From the text: Robin leaves London without permission, breaking his pardon.
What does Robin's defiance of the King's wishes cost him?
3. FUGITIVE  (noun/adjective) — a person who has escaped from captivity or is in hiding
From the text: Robin becomes a fugitive again the moment he leaves London.
How is being a fugitive different now than when Robin was eighteen?
4. NOSTALGIA  (noun) — a sentimental longing for the past; homesickness
vocabulary.contextSentence: And for a while, the old days returned.
vocabulary.discussionQuestion: Why does the phrase "for a while" undercut the nostalgia?

choices: He risks losing his friendship with Little John
He risks breaking his pardon and becoming a wanted outlaw again

language.instruction: Idioms mean something different from the literal words.

informationalText.title: Medieval Freedom and Happiness
informationalText.text:
In medieval England, most people were tied to the land where they were born. Serfs couldn't leave their lord's estate without permission. Even freemen needed permission to travel or change occupations. "Freedom" meant something different than it does today.

Robin Hood's band represents a radical idea: freedom matters more than security or wealth. They choose danger and hardship in the forest over safety and servitude in society. The story asks: What makes a good life? Is it better to be safe and controlled, or free and at risk?

journalPrompt.prompt: Ellen was being forced to marry someone she didn't love to pay her father's debt. Do children owe their parents absolute obedience, or are there times when it's right to refuse?

chapters.title: THE SHERIFF'S LAST STAND
chapters.summary: The new Sheriff was a different kind of enemy — methodical, patient, and cruel.  He didn't set traps or hold archery contests.
chapters.text:
The new Sheriff was a different kind of enemy — methodical, patient, and cruel. He didn't set traps or hold archery contests. Instead, he burned the villages that supported Robin. He arrested families. He offered rewards so large that even loyal friends were tempted to betray the band.
One by one, the safe houses disappeared. The network of farmers and servants that Marian had built was dismantled through fear and force. Supplies grew scarce. The band was forced deeper into the forest, into territory that was harder to defend and harder to live in.
Then the Sheriff gathered an army — not a patrol, not an escort, but a real army of two hundred soldiers — and marched into Sherwood Forest.
The battle that followed was the largest Robin had ever fought. His men were outnumbered three to one, but they knew the forest. They used every trick: false trails, hidden pits, rope traps, ambushes from the trees. Arrows rained down from invisible positions. Soldiers who chased shadows found themselves lost in the green maze of Sherwood.
Little John held a narrow bridge alone for thirty minutes, his quarterstaff cracking against swords and shields until the soldiers retreated rather than face him. Marian led a flanking attack that drove an entire company into a bog. Will Scarlet fought in his red outfit like a flame moving through the trees.
By nightfall, the Sheriff's army withdrew. They had lost dozens of men and accomplished nothing. Robin had won — but the cost was high. Several of his men were wounded. Their camp was destroyed. Their supplies were gone.
Robin sat on a stump, exhausted, watching his people tend their wounds and rebuild their shelters. He was forty years old. His hair was beginning to gray. The wound on his arm from the silver arrow contest still ached in cold weather.
"How long can we keep doing this?" he asked Marian.
She didn't answer. She didn't have to.

vocabulary.discussionQuestion: What is lost when the support network is dismantled?

vocabulary.definition:
relating to a type of warfare using small groups, surprise attacks, and knowledge of local terrain
From the text: Robin's men use guerrilla tactics — false trails, hidden pits, ambushes from trees.
Why are guerrilla tactics effective against a larger army?
4. TOLL  (noun) — the cost or damage resulting from something; also, a charge for passage
vocabulary.contextSentence: The cost was high.
vocabulary.discussionQuestion: What physical and emotional toll has years of fighting taken on Robin?

choices: He is kinder and tries to negotiate with Robin
He is methodical and patient — he destroys Robin's support network before attacking

choices: Robin wins decisively with no losses

informationalText.title: Medieval Warfare Tactics
informationalText.text:
Medieval armies fought in formation with heavy cavalry and infantry. But forest warfare was different. In dense woods, horses were useless, formations couldn't form, and visibility was poor. This favored defenders who knew the terrain.

Robin's band used guerrilla tactics: ambushes, hit-and-run attacks, hidden pits, rope traps, and arrows from concealment. They fought only when conditions favored them and melted away when they didn't. This style of warfare frustrated traditional armies and made even small bands of outlaws difficult to defeat.

chapters.title: BETRAYAL
chapters.summary:
The betrayal came from within. 
A man named Roger of Doncaster had joined the band six months earlier.
chapters.text:
The betrayal came from within.
A man named Roger of Doncaster had joined the band six months earlier. He was clever and capable, and he had quickly made himself useful. Robin trusted him. That was Robin's nature — he saw the best in people, even when the best wasn't there.
Roger was a spy. The Sheriff had planted him in the band, paying his family handsomely in exchange for information. For months, Roger had been reporting everything — the location of the camp, the patrol routes, the identities of Robin's supporters in the villages.
The Sheriff used this information to plan a devastating strike. Not at Robin directly — at his support network. In a single night, the Sheriff's men arrested twenty people who had been helping the outlaws: farmers who hid weapons, women who passed messages, a blacksmith who repaired their arrows.
The arrests shattered the band's connection to the outside world. Worse, they shattered Robin's faith. When the truth about Roger came out — discovered by Marian, who had always been wary of the newcomer — Robin was devastated.
"I brought him into our family," Robin said. "I vouched for him."
"You trusted someone," Marian said gently. "That's not a weakness. It's who you are."
"It's who I was," Robin said, and his voice was older than his years.
Roger escaped into the night before Robin's men could catch him. The people who were arrested were eventually released — the Sheriff had no real evidence — but the damage was done. The invisible web that connected Robin Hood to the people of Nottinghamshire was torn, and it could not be easily mended.

informationalText.title: Medieval Betrayal and Trust
informationalText.text:
In medieval society, trust was based on oaths and honor. People swore loyalty to lords, who promised protection in return. Breaking these oaths was the worst crime—worse than theft or violence. A traitor was despised because they destroyed the social bonds that held communities together.

Robin's band operated on trust. They had no legal contracts, no written agreements—only their word. Roger's betrayal shatters this foundation. It shows that even the most careful leader can be deceived, and that rebuilding trust after betrayal is nearly impossible.

writingPrompt.prompt: The Merry Men rescue Allan and Ellen's wedding. Write about a time when someone helped you in a difficult situation, or when you helped someone else. What happened, and how did it feel?

chapters.title: THE PRIORESS
chapters.summary: Robin was not well.  The wound on his arm had never healed properly, and now a fever had taken hold — burning and relentless, sapping his strength day by day.
chapters.text:
Robin was not well. The wound on his arm had never healed properly, and now a fever had taken hold — burning and relentless, sapping his strength day by day. Friar Tuck's medicines helped, but they couldn't cure what ailed him.
"You need proper care," Marian said. "A physician. Medicine. A real bed."
Robin resisted, but eventually his body overruled his will. He could barely stand.
Marian knew of a place: Kirklees Priory, a convent run by Robin's cousin, the Prioress. In those days, convents and monasteries were often the only places with medical knowledge. The nuns practiced bloodletting — the common treatment for fever — and the Prioress had a reputation as a healer.
Robin rode to Kirklees with Little John at his side. They arrived at the priory as the sun was setting. The building was old and cold, built of gray stone, surrounded by a high wall. The Prioress met them at the gate.
She was a thin woman with sharp eyes and a smile that didn't reach them. She welcomed Robin warmly — too warmly, Little John thought. Something felt wrong, but Robin was too ill to notice.
"Let me bleed you," the Prioress said, leading Robin to a private chamber. "The fever must be drawn out."
Little John wanted to stay, but Robin waved him off. "Wait outside. I'll be fine."
Little John waited. An hour passed. Two hours. Three. The sun set. The moon rose. No word came from inside.
Something was very wrong.
Little John pounded on the door. No answer. He broke it down with one massive kick.
Robin lay on the bed, pale as death. His arm was bandaged, but the bandage was soaked through. The Prioress had opened a vein and left it bleeding — not to heal, but to kill. She had been paid by the Sheriff. Robin's own cousin had betrayed him.
"John," Robin whispered. His voice was barely audible. "My bow. Bring me my bow."
