informationalText.title: Medieval Medicine: Bloodletting
informationalText.text:
Medieval medicine was based on the theory of "humors"—four bodily fluids (blood, phlegm, yellow bile, black bile) that needed to be balanced. Bloodletting was a common treatment: cutting a vein to release "excess" blood, which supposedly cured fevers, infections, and other ailments.

In reality, bloodletting often did more harm than good, especially when patients were already weak. The Prioress's use of bloodletting to murder Robin while claiming to heal him shows how medical procedures could be weapons. Patients trusted healers completely, making them vulnerable to betrayal.

journalPrompt.prompt: Marian says Robin is getting reckless because his pride makes him show off. If someone you cared about was making dangerous decisions, would you tell them? How do you balance caring about someone with respecting their choices?

chapters.title: THE LAST ARROW
chapters.summary: Little John carried Robin to the window.  The room overlooked a green meadow that sloped down to the edge of Sherwood Forest.
chapters.text:
Little John carried Robin to the window. The room overlooked a green meadow that sloped down to the edge of Sherwood Forest. The ancient oaks stood dark against the evening sky, their branches reaching upward like arms.
Robin's great longbow was placed in his hands. He could barely hold it. The strength that had drawn that bow ten thousand times — that had won tournaments, defended the poor, and defied the most powerful men in England — was nearly gone.
"Open the window," Robin said.
Little John opened it. Cool forest air rushed in, carrying the scent of earth and leaves and rain. Robin breathed it in — the smell of home.
"Wherever this arrow falls," Robin said, his voice thin but steady, "bury me there."
He nocked the arrow. His hands trembled. Little John steadied his arm.
Robin drew the bow — not with the full power of his youth, but with everything he had left. He aimed high, toward the green line of the forest, and released.
The arrow flew.
It arced across the twilight sky, a dark line against the fading light, and sailed over the meadow in a long, graceful curve. It cleared the stone wall of the priory, crossed the open ground, and disappeared into the trees at the forest's edge.
Robin watched it fly. A faint smile crossed his face.
"There," he whispered. "Bury me there. Under the greenwood tree."
He closed his eyes. Little John held him. The great outlaw of Sherwood Forest — the archer, the trickster, the champion of the poor — was gone.

comprehension.question: What does Robin ask Little John to do with his final arrow?
choices: Deliver it to the King as proof that Robin Hood has died
Bury Robin wherever the arrow lands
Shoot the arrow at the Prioress as punishment for her betrayal
Keep the arrow as a reminder of their friendship

comprehension.question: Why does Robin shoot toward the forest rather than in any other direction?
choices: The forest is the closest target and he is too weak to shoot far
The forest has been his true home, and he wants to rest at its edge forever
He is aiming at a specific tree where he hid treasure
Little John tells him to aim that direction

comprehension.question: Robin's last act is to shoot an arrow. Why is this the perfect ending for his character? What does the arrow symbolize about Robin's life, identity, and legacy?

grammar.instruction: Possessive nouns show ownership. Add 's to singular, ' to plural ending in s.

informationalText.title: Medieval Death and Burial
informationalText.text:
In medieval England, death rituals were deeply important. Bodies were prepared by family, buried quickly (usually within a day), and laid to rest in churchyards or significant locations. Outlaws couldn't be buried in consecrated ground—their deaths were unmarked, their graves forgotten.

Robin's burial at the edge of the forest, marked by his last arrow's flight, creates a legend instead of a forgotten grave. The choice of burial place matters: not in a church controlled by corrupt clergy, not in a town that rejected him, but at the boundary between civilization and wildness—where he always belonged.

writingPrompt.prompt: Robin's pride and love of showing off keep getting him into danger. Everyone has a quality that is both a strength and a weakness. What's yours? Give specific examples of how it helps you and how it sometimes causes problems.

chapters.title: THE LEGEND
chapters.summary: They buried Robin Hood where his arrow fell — at the edge of Sherwood Forest, beneath an ancient oak tree, with the green canopy overhead and the open meadow stretching away to the south.  Little John dug the grave himself.
chapters.text:
They buried Robin Hood where his arrow fell — at the edge of Sherwood Forest, beneath an ancient oak tree, with the green canopy overhead and the open meadow stretching away to the south. Little John dug the grave himself. Marian laid Robin's bow beside him.
The Merry Men gathered for the last time. Little John. Friar Tuck. Will Scarlet. Allan-a-Dale. And dozens of others — the men and women who had lived and fought and laughed together in the greenwood for more than twenty years.
Allan-a-Dale played his lute. No one spoke. The music said everything.
Marian stood at the grave for a long time after the others had gone. She touched the fresh earth with her hand and whispered something that no one else heard. Then she walked into the forest and disappeared among the trees.
In the weeks that followed, the band scattered. There was no one who could replace Robin Hood — not because no one was brave enough or skilled enough, but because Robin had been the one thing that held them all together. He was the idea that had made the greenwood more than just a hiding place for outlaws.
Little John went north. Some say he lived to be very old. Friar Tuck returned to the church — a small, poor church in a farming village, where he served the people Robin had always fought for. Will Scarlet traveled to the continent, still wearing his scarlet. Allan-a-Dale wandered England, singing songs about Robin Hood at every fair and market and manor house that would have him.
And the songs survived.
Long after the oaks of Sherwood Forest had grown old and fallen and been replaced by new trees, the story of Robin Hood lived on. Farmers told it to their children. Minstrels sang it in great halls and village squares. Scholars wrote it down. And every person who heard the tale understood the same simple truth that Robin had lived by:
The strong should protect the weak. The rich owe a debt to the poor. Courage means standing up for what is right, even when the powerful say it is wrong. And no law written by a tyrant is more just than the law written in a good person's heart.
Robin Hood is buried at the edge of Sherwood Forest, beneath the greenwood tree, with his great bow beside him and an arrow's flight between him and the world.
But the legend never rests. The legend walks the roads of England still, looking for injustice, ready to fight, and always — always — wearing Lincoln green.
THE END

informationalText.title: How Legends Survive
informationalText.text:
The Robin Hood legend has survived for over 700 years through ballads, plays, and stories. Why? Because it addresses timeless themes: rich vs. poor, justice vs. law, freedom vs. security, individual vs. authority. Every generation reinterprets the legend for its own time.

Historians debate whether Robin Hood was real. Some evidence suggests several real outlaws might have inspired the legend. But historical accuracy misses the point: Robin Hood is real as an idea. He represents the hope that someone will stand up to injustice, that the powerful can be challenged, and that one person with courage can make a difference.

journalPrompt.prompt: The Sheriff's second trap comes closer to catching Robin than the first. Why do you think it's harder for Robin to escape this time? What does this teach us about how people learn from their failures?
